student_message: Oh cool! Infinite fold lines means we could slice a pizza into a million perfectly matching pieces! What if the dough gets stretched into an oval shape, how many fold lines does it have then?
final_message:
You have such a fun way of thinking about math, Zara! If you stretch that perfect pizza dough into an oval, it loses its infinite fold lines. Instead, an oval has exactly two lines of symmetry. 

Imagine folding that stretched oval dough. You can fold it straight down the middle from top to bottom, making a perfect mirror image on the left and right. You can also fold it straight across from side to side, making a perfect mirror image on the top and bottom. If you try folding it diagonally, the curved edges will not line up perfectly anymore. So, your stretched pizza dough has exactly two special fold lines! 🍕✨ 

What other fun shapes should we try to fold next?

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain that an oval has exactly two lines of symmetry.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student is happy, focused, and asked a specific conceptual question about ovals. The dialogue agent should answer her question and explain the concept.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
session_summary: Zara covered rotational and reflectional symmetry, exploring fold lines in letters, triangles, circles, and ovals. She understands these concepts exceptionally well, accurately deducing symmetry for various triangles and grasping infinite symmetry in circles. Zara exhibited no struggles. Her learning state is highly engaged and progressive; having mastered basic 2D symmetry, she readily applies concepts to real-world objects and proactively asks advanced questions about stretched shapes and 3D objects.
